what: Reading all images from a folder
title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: can start analyzing images, need load R. example, choose read images shipped imagefluency package. However, can replace file path file path computer. Next, read file names images satisfy specific pattern using list.files(). case, want file names files .jpg file extension. Thus, specify pattern = '*.jpg$'. $ sign end ensures find files end .jpg, file like image1.jpg.png therefore listed. Multiple patterns also specified (e.g., pattern = '*.jpg$|*.png$'). Note actually load images R session . Instead, using list.files() just extract images’ file paths later use.
code: # path to images (here: example images from the imagefluency package) #  # --NOTE: replace with your folder of interest, e.g. #         mypath <- "C:/Users/NAME/Documents/Project/images/"  mypath <- system.file("example_images", package = "imagefluency")  # read filenames of images # --NOTE: change file ending accordingly! fileNames <- list.files(mypath, pattern = '*.jpg$', full.names = TRUE)

title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: next step, load imagefluency package create data frame store results. tutorial, ’ll use simple loop iterate images folder, get image fluency scores, write results data frame. simplest approach many images might run memory issues loading images . course, also possible run analyses using lapply() purrr::map() parallel counterparts (see speeding analysis).
code: # load imagefluency package library(imagefluency)  # data frame to store results  results <- data.frame(filename = basename(fileNames),                       contrast = NA, selfsimilarity = NA, simplicity = NA,                       symmetry = NA, typicality = NA)

title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: Now ’s time calculate image fluency scores! end, create big loop iterates image files. Within loop, first, image read R. Next scores contrast, self-similarity, simplicity, symmetry computed image. results stored results data frame created . use tryCatch() loop loop break errors specific image. Instead, result simply NA error. Note compute vertical symmetry code . Optionally, can also convert images grayscale speed computation.
code: # big loop over all images for(i in seq_along(fileNames)){   # print loop info   cat('** Estimating scores for image',i,'**\n')      # 1. read image into R   img <- img_read(fileNames[i])      # 2. convert image to grayscale to speed up computation (optional)   # --NOTE: remove comment sign in the following line to apply grayscale conversion   # img <- rgb2gray(img)      # 3. estimate image fluency scores (except typicality)   #    and store the results   results$contrast[i] <- tryCatch(img_contrast(img), error = function(e) NA)   results$selfsimilarity[i] <- tryCatch(img_self_similarity(img), error = function(e) NA)   results$simplicity[i] <- tryCatch(img_simplicity(fileNames[i]), error = function(e) NA)   results$symmetry[i] <- tryCatch(img_symmetry(img, horizontal = FALSE), error = function(e) NA) } > ** Estimating scores for image 1 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 2 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 3 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 4 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 5 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 6 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 7 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 8 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 9 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 10 ** > ** Estimating scores for image 11 **

title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: might noticed didn’t compute typicality code . reason typicality characteristic single image , can computed relative set images. Thus, use loop construct . Instead, read images memory compute typicality. Note depending number size images, might run memory problems. ’s case, either use computer larger memory reduce image set accordingly.
code: # 4. get image typicality scores # --NOTE: image typicality is estimated relative to all other images, hence, #         the following might take quite a while #  # first read all images at once # -- NOTE: if you have too many images, you might run into memory problems allImages <- lapply(fileNames, img_read)  # now estimate typicality and store the results (only numeric vector without names) results$typicality <- as.vector(img_typicality(allImages))

title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: done! now computed image fluency scores. Let’s look results. everything worked, can save results e.g. .csv file.
code: knitr::kable(results, digits = 3) # save the results into the image folder write.csv(results, file = paste0(mypath, 'imagefluency_scores.csv'), row.names = FALSE)

title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: plan include typicality image analysis next metrics, need big loop . reason load images memory computing typicality anyway. Thus, can also step right beginning analysis compute image fluency scores .

what: Reading all images into memory using lapply()
title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: example , ’ll use base R’s lapply() function read images. However, can course use purrr::map() just well. Using lapply() simple: tell R apply img_read() function every element fileNames object. result list every list element contains image matrix. Thus, object might big! code works perfectly fine every image can read without problems (usually case). However, want robust version, can also define custom img_read() function includes error handling tryCatch.
code: # read all images at once into memory allImages <- lapply(fileNames, img_read) # define custom image read function that catches errors and returns NA img_read_no_error <- function(img) {   tryCatch(img_read(img), error=function(e) NA) }  # read images with custom function allImages <- lapply(fileNames, img_read_no_error)

what: Computing image fluency scores using lapply()
title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: images read memory, can compute image fluency scores images. facilitate process contrast, self-similarity, simplicity, symmetry, define function computes scores given image, apply function images. ’s done, can convert results data frame add images’ file names. final step, add typicality scores previous section look results.
code: # define function that computes all image fluency scores except typicality img_fluency_scores <- function(img) {   contr <- tryCatch(img_contrast(img), error = function(e) NA)   selfsim <- tryCatch(img_self_similarity(img), error = function(e) NA)   simpl <- tryCatch(img_simplicity(img), error = function(e) NA)   sym <- tryCatch(img_symmetry(img, horizontal = FALSE), error = function(e) NA)   return(c(contrast = contr,                selfsimilarity = selfsim,               simplicity = simpl,               symmetry = unname(sym))) }  # apply function to images results <- lapply(allImages, img_fluency_scores) # convert results to data frame and add file names results <- do.call(rbind, results) results <- data.frame(filename = basename(fileNames), results) # compute and add typicality to results results$typicality <- as.vector(img_typicality(allImages))  # print a nicely formatted results table knitr::kable(results, digits = 3)

title: Analyzing multiple images at once
text: code using lapply() faster approach using first big loop reading images compute typicality. However, might gain much larger increase speed, using parallelized versions lapply() purrr::map(), respectively. following code example demonstrates shortly achieve . Essentially, replace instances lapply() parallel::mclapply() (multi-core lapply). example , however, ’ll use pbmcapply::pbmclapply adds progress bar (pb) multi-core lapply. Let’s quick comparison computing time computing four image fluency scores without multi-core processing. Note increasing file size number images, speed bump become larger. ‘simulate’ reading images 10 times. can see multi-core approach  three times faster (single test run) single-core approach! Let’s see furrr::future_map() compares . Finally, confirm results .
code: # detect cores for multi-core processing ncores <- parallel::detectCores()  # read images with multi-core lapply and progress bar allImages <- pbmcapply::pbmclapply(fileNames, img_read_no_error, mc.cores = ncores)  # compute image fluency scores  results <- pbmcapply::pbmclapply(allImages, img_fluency_scores, mc.cores = ncores)  # convert results to data frame and add file names results <- do.call(rbind, results) results <- data.frame(filename = basename(fileNames), results)  # compute and add typicality to results results$typicality <- as.vector(img_typicality(allImages)) # read images 10 times  allImages <- lapply(rep(fileNames, 10), img_read_no_error) cat('Number of images:', length(allImages), '\n') > Number of images: 110 # compute imgagefluency scores using lapply() with progress bar tictoc::tic("lapply") results_lapply <- pbapply::pblapply(allImages, img_fluency_scores) tictoc::toc(log=TRUE) > lapply: 8.73 sec elapsed # compute imgagefluency scores using multi-core lapply() with progress bar tictoc::tic("pbmclapply") results_pbmclapply <- pbmcapply::pbmclapply(allImages, img_fluency_scores,                                             mc.cores = ncores) tictoc::toc(log=TRUE) > pbmclapply: 2.44 sec elapsed library(dplyr) future::plan('multisession')  tictoc::tic('future_map') results_future_map <- allImages %>% furrr::future_map(img_fluency_scores,                                       .progress = TRUE,                                       .options = furrr::furrr_options(seed = TRUE)) tictoc::toc(log=TRUE) > future_map: 2.98 sec elapsed all.equal(results_lapply, results_pbmclapply) > [1] TRUE all.equal(results_lapply, results_future_map) > [1] TRUE

what: Motivation: Why create yet another R package
title: Getting started
text: last decades, amount data generated growing rapidly, predominantly due digitalization. today’s data unstructured, share increasing. Given unstructured data rich, data provide rich insights scientific research variety fields practice alike. Especially images (.e., visual stimuli) recognized valuable source information. time, vision research research psychology shows even simple changes low-level image features (like symmetry, contrast, complexity) can tremendous effect variety human judgments. example, statement like “Nut bread healthier potato bread” likely perceived true presented color easy read white background (high contrast) instead presented color difficult read white background (low contrast; cf. [1]). Thus, might useful estimate control differences low-level visual features research includes visual stimuli. imagefluency R package low-level image scores based processing fluency theory. package allows get scores several basic aesthetic principles facilitate fluent cognitive processing images: contrast, complexity / simplicity, self-similarity, symmetry, typicality.

what: Why and when to use the package
title: Getting started
text: Possible applications include: stimulus selection experiments (e.g., testing brand logos, evaluate product designs, online display ads) control variables statistical prediction models linking image fluency scores outcomes interest (e.g., typical product packaging look like, simpler images get less attention website, …) (interpretable) image features simple machine learning models, e.g. SVM image classifier

title: Getting started
text: prevailing explanation low-level image features affect human judgments based processing fluency theory [2]. Processing fluency describes ease processing stimulus [3], happens instantaneously automatically [4]. Higher processing fluency results gut-level positive affective response [5]. Notably, rich body literature shown processing fluency effects impact variety judgmental domains everyday life, including much like things, much consider statements true, trustworthy judge person, risky think something , whether buy product (review, see [6]). Several stimulus features proposed result increased fluency. particular, visual symmetry, simplicity, (proto-)typicality, contrast identified facilitate processing [2]. Recent studies discuss self-similarity light fluency-based aesthetics [7, 8], concept studied example images natural scenes [9]. Self-similarity can described self-repeating patterns within stimulus. typical example leaves ferns feature shape regardless magnification reduction (.e., scale invariance). Another prominent example romanesco broccoli self-similar surface. Extracting image features contrast, self-similarity, simplicity, symmetry, typicality therefore constitute core purpose imagefluency package.

title: Getting started
text: img_contrast() visual contrast image img_complexity() visual complexity image (opposite simplicity) img_self_similarity() visual self-similarity image img_simplicity() visual simplicity image (opposite complexity) img_symmetry() vertical horizontal symmetry image img_typicality() visual typicality list images relative

title: Getting started
text: img_read() reads bitmap images R rgb2gray() converts images RGB grayscale (might speed computation) run_imagefluency() launch Shiny app locally computer interactive demo main functions (alternatively, visit online version posit.cloud)

title: Getting started
text: can install current stable version CRAN. download latest development version Github use install_github function remotes package. installation, imagefluency package loaded usual way calling library(imagefluency). img_read() function can used read image R. Just like reading dataset, img_read() expects path file input, e.g., img_read('C:/Users/myname/Documents/myimage.jpg'). Currently supported file formats bmp, jpg, png, tif. Use following link report bugs/issues: https://github.com/stm/imagefluency/issues
code: install.packages('imagefluency') # install remotes if necessary if (!require('remotes')) install.packages('remotes') # install imagefluency from github remotes::install_github('stm/imagefluency')

title: Getting started
text: imagefluency allows get scores five image features facilitate fluent processing images: contrast, complexity / simplicity, self-similarity, symmetry, typicality. use imagefluency package, first load library.
code: library(imagefluency)

title: Getting started
text: function img_contrast() returns contrast image. research defines contrast images root-mean-squared (RMS) contrast standard deviation normalized pixel intensity values [10]: 1MN∑=0N−1∑j=0M−1(Iij−‾)2\sqrt{\frac{1}{M N}\sum_{=0}^{N-1}\sum_{j=0}^{M - 1}(I_{ij} - \bar{})^2}. RMS image measure visual contrast shown predict human contrast detection thresholds well [11]. Therefore, function calculates contrast computing RMS contrast input image. Consequently, higher value indicates higher contrast. image normalized necessary (.e., normalization range [0, 1]). color images, weighted average color channels computed (cf. [8]). Note following, example images come package used. Moreover, images can displayed using grid.raster() function grid package. Calculating contrast scores two images gives following result: score: 0.08 score: 0.29
code: # Example image with relatively high contrast: berries berries <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'berries.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # display image grid::grid.raster(berries) # get contrast img_contrast(berries) # Example image with relatively low contrast: bike bike <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'bike.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # display image grid::grid.raster(bike) # get contrast img_contrast(bike)

title: Getting started
text: function img_complexity() returns visual complexity image. Algorithmic information theory indicates picture complexity can measured accurately image compression rates complex images denser fewer redundancies [12, 13]. Therefore, function calculates visual complexity image ratio compressed uncompressed image file size. Thus, value depend image size. function takes file path image file (URL) pre-loaded image input argument returns ratio compressed divided uncompressed image file size. complexity values naturally interpretable can range almost 0 (virtually completely compressed image, thus extremely simple image) 1 (compression possible, thus extremely complex image). function offers use different image compression algorithms like jpg, gif, png algorithm = 'zip' default (discussion different algorithms, see [8]). compression algorithms depict horizontal vertical redundancies equally, function includes optional rotate parameter (default: FALSE). Setting parameter TRUE additionally creates compressed version rotated image. overall compressed image’s file size computed minimum original image’s file size file size rotated image. function img_simplicity() returns visual simplicity image. Image simplicity complement image complexity therefore calculated 1 minus complexity score (.e., compression rate). Values can range 0 (compression possible, thus extremely complex image) almost 1 (virtually completely compressed image, thus extremely simple image). Calculating complexity scores two images gives following result: score: 0.89 score: 0.42
code: # Example image with high complexity: trees trees <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'trees.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # display image grid::grid.raster(trees) # get complexity img_complexity(trees) # Example image with low complexity: sky sky <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'sky.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # display image grid::grid.raster(sky) # get complexity img_complexity(sky)

title: Getting started
text: function img_self_similarity() returns self-similarity image. Self-similarity can measured Fourier power spectrum image. Previous research identified spectral power natural scenes falls spatial frequencies (ff) according power law (1fp\frac{1}{f^p}) values pp near value 2, indicates scale invariance (review, see [14]). Therefore, function computes self-similarity via slope log-log power spectrum image using OLS. value self-similarity returned function calculated self-similarity=|slope+2|*(−1)\text{self-similarity} = |\text{slope} + 2| * (-1). , measure reaches maximum value 0 slope −2-2, deviation −2-2 results negative values negative higher deviation −2-2. Thus, range self-similarity scores −∞-\infty 00. color images, weighted average color channel’s values computed. possible get raw regression slope (instead transformed value indicates self-similarity) using option raw = TRUE. options include possibility plot log-log power spectrum (logplot = TRUE) base computation slope full frequency spectrum (full = TRUE). See function’s help file details (.e., ?img_self_similarity). Calculating self-similarity scores two images gives result . score romanesco broccoli much closer maximum possible value 0, hence much self-similar. score: -0.03 score: -1.22
code: # Example image with high self-similarity: romanesco romanesco <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'romanesco.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # display image grid::grid.raster(romanesco) # get self-similarity img_self_similarity(romanesco) # Example image with low self-similarity: office office <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'office.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # display image grid::grid.raster(office) # get self-similarity img_self_similarity(office)

title: Getting started
text: function img_symmetry() returns vertical horizontal symmetry image numeric value 0 (symmetrical) 1 (perfectly symmetrical). Symmetry computed correlation corresponding image halves (.e., pairwise correlation corresponding pixels, cf. [15]). perceptual mirror axis necessarily exactly middle picture, function detects first step ‘optimal’ mirror axis estimating several symmetry values different positions mirror axis. end, mirror axis automatically shifted 5% right (case vertical symmetry; analogously horizontal symmetry). second step, overall symmetry score computed maximum symmetry scores given different mirror axes. color images, weighted average color channel’s values computed. See [8] details. function two optional logical parameters: vertical horizontal (TRUE default). one parameter set FALSE, vertical horizontal symmetry computed, respectively. See function’s help file (.e., ?img_symmetry) information additional options shift_range per_channel. Calculating vertical symmetry scores two images gives following result: score: 0.96 score: 0.48
code: # Example image with high vertical symmetry: rails rails <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'rails.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # display image grid::grid.raster(rails) # get only vertical symmetry img_symmetry(rails, horizontal = FALSE) # Example image with low vertical symmetry: bridge bridge <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'bridge.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # display image grid::grid.raster(bridge) # get only vertical symmetry img_symmetry(bridge, horizontal = FALSE)

title: Getting started
text: function img_typicality() returns visual typicality set images relative . Values can range -1 (inversely typical) 0 (typical) 1 (perfectly typical). , higher absolute values indicate larger typicality. typicality score computed correlation particular image average representation images, .e., mean images [16]. , typicality image can assessed isolation, comparison set images category. color images, weighted average color channel’s values computed. images different dimensions automatically resized smallest height width. Rescaling images prior computing typicality scores can specified optional rescaling parameter (must numeric value). rescaling possible assess typicality different perceptual levels (see [17] details). users won’t need rescaling can use default (rescale = NULL). following example shows three images two depict valleys mountains one depicts fireworks. Therefore, fireworks image comparison rather low typicality set images (.e., atypical). important note image’s typicality score highly depends reference set image compared . Calculating typicality scores three images gives following result: score: 0.75 score: 0.40 score: 0.72
code: # Example images depicting valleys: valley_white, valley_green # Example image depicting fireworks: fireworks valley_white <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'valley_white.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) valley_green <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'valley_green.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) fireworks <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'fireworks.jpg', package = 'imagefluency'))  # create image set as list imglist <- list(valley_white, fireworks, valley_green)  # get typicality img_typicality(imglist)

title: Getting started
text: imagefluency simple R package image fluency scores. package allows get scores several basic aesthetic principles facilitate fluent cognitive processing images. straightforward use allows easy conversion unstructured data structured image features. structured image features naturally interpretable (.e., black-box-model). Finally, including image information statistical models might increase model’s statistical predictive power.

title: Image Statistics Based on Processing Fluency
text: imagefluency R package image fluency scores. package allows get scores several basic aesthetic principles facilitate fluent cognitive processing images. main functions : img_contrast() get visual contrast image. img_complexity() get visual complexity image (equals 1 minus image simplicity) img_self_similarity() get visual self-similarity image img_simplicity() function get visual simplicity image (equals 1 minus image complexity). img_symmetry() get vertical horizontal symmetry image. img_typicality() get visual typicality list images relative helpful functions : img_read() wrapper function read images R using read.bitmap() readbitmap package rgb2gray() convert images RGB grayscale (might speed computation) run_imagefluency() launch Shiny app locally computer interactive demo main functions main author Stefan Mayer.

title: Image Statistics Based on Processing Fluency
text: interactive dashboard available analyze images without writing code (see screenshot ). dashboard supports multi-image uploads, side--side comparison, CSV export.  can run dashboard locally imagefluency::run_imagefluency() package installed, try online .

title: Image Statistics Based on Processing Fluency
text: can install current stable version CRAN. download latest development version Github use install_github function remotes package. Optionally, rmarkdown installed, can also system build vignettes downloading GitHub. Use following link report bugs/issues: https://github.com/stm/imagefluency/issues
code: install.packages('imagefluency') # install remotes if necessary if (!require('remotes')) install.packages('remotes') # install imagefluency from github remotes::install_github('stm/imagefluency') # install from github with vignettes (needs rmarkdown installed) remotes::install_github('stm/imagefluency', build_vignettes = TRUE)

title: Image Statistics Based on Processing Fluency
code: # visual contrast # # example image file (from package): bike.jpg bike_location <- system.file('example_images', 'bike.jpg', package = 'imagefluency') # read image from file bike <- img_read(bike_location) # get contrast img_contrast(bike)  # visual symmetry # # read image rails <- img_read(system.file('example_images', 'rails.jpg', package = 'imagefluency')) # get only vertical symmetry img_symmetry(rails, horizontal = FALSE)

Psychology Aesthetics, Creativity, Arts, 12(4), 399–431. doi: 10.1037/aca0000187 Mayer, S. & Landwehr, J. R. (2018). Objective measures design typicality. Design Studies, 54, 146–161. doi: 10.1016/j.destud.2017.09.004

See Mayer & Landwehr (2018) doi:10.1037/aca0000187  Mayer & Landwehr (2018) doi:10.31219/osf.io/gtbhw  theoretical background methods.

title: imagefluency: Image Statistics Based on Processing Fluency — imagefluency-package
text: main functions : img_contrast get visual contrast   image img_complexity get visual complexity   image (equals 1 minus image simplicity) img_self_similarity get visual   self-similarity image img_simplicity get visual simplicity   image (equals 1 minus image complexity) img_symmetry get vertical   horizontal symmetry image img_typicality get visual typicality   list images relative helpful functions : img_read wrapper function   read images using readbitmap::read.bitmap run_imagefluency launch Shiny app   interactive demo main functions rgb2gray convert images   RGB grayscale

title: imagefluency: Image Statistics Based on Processing Fluency — imagefluency-package
text: Maintainer: Stefan Mayer stefan@mayer-de.com (ORCID)

title: Image complexity — img_complexity
text: img_complexity returns complexity image via image compression. Higher values indicate higher image complexity.

title: Image complexity — img_complexity
code: img_complexity(imgfile, algorithm = "zip", rotate = FALSE)

title: Image complexity — img_complexity
text: imgfile Either character string containing path image file (URL) image form matrix (grayscale image) array (color image) numeric values representing pre-loaded image (e.g. using img_read()). algorithm Character string specifies image compression algorithm use. Currently implemented zip deflate compression (default), jpg, gif, png. rotate logical. compressed file size rotated image also computed? (see details)

title: Image complexity — img_complexity
text: numeric value: ratio compressed divided   uncompressed image file size

title: Image complexity — img_complexity
text: function returns visual complexity image. Visual   complexity calculated ratio compressed uncompressed   image file size. Preferably, original image uncompressed image   file. function takes file path image file (URL) pre-loaded   image input argument (imgfile) returns ratio   compressed divided uncompressed image file size. Values can range   almost 0 (virtually completely compressed image, thus extremely   simple image) 1 (compression possible, thus extremely complex   image). can choose different image compression algorithms. Currently   implemented zip deflate compression (default), jpg,   gif, png. See Mayer & Landwehr (2018) discussion   different image compression algorithms measuring visual complexity. compression algorithms depict horizontal vertical   redundancies equally, function includes optional rotate   parameter (default: FALSE). Setting parameter TRUE   following effects: first, image rotated 90 degrees.   Second, compressed version rotated image created. Finally,   overall compressed image's file size computed minimum   original image's file size file size rotated image. R's built-bmp device creates () indexed instead   True Color images (b) creates files different file sizes depending   operating system, function relies magick package   write (read) images.

title: Image complexity — img_complexity
text: Donderi, D. C. (2006). Visual complexity: Review.   Psychological Bulletin, 132, 73–97.   doi:10.1037/0033-2909.132.1.73 Forsythe, ., Nadal, M., Sheehy, N., Cela-Conde, C. J., & Sawey,   M. (2011). Predicting Beauty: Fractal Dimension Visual Complexity   Art. British Journal Psychology, 102, 49–70.   doi:10.1348/000712610X498958 Mayer, S. & Landwehr, J, R. (2018). Quantifying Visual Aesthetics   Based Processing Fluency Theory: Four Algorithmic Measures   Antecedents Aesthetic Preferences. Psychology Aesthetics,   Creativity, Arts, 12(4), 399–431.   doi:10.1037/aca0000187

title: Image complexity — img_complexity
code: # Example image with high complexity: trees trees <- img_read(system.file("example_images", "trees.jpg", package = "imagefluency")) # # display image grid::grid.raster(trees) # # get complexity img_complexity(trees) #> [1] 0.8949686   # Example image with low complexity: sky sky <- img_read(system.file("example_images", "sky.jpg", package = "imagefluency")) # # display image grid::grid.raster(sky)  # # get complexity img_complexity(sky) #> [1] 0.4199254

title: Image contrast — img_contrast

title: Image contrast — img_contrast
text: function returns RMS contrast image img.   RMS contrast defined standard deviation normalized pixel   intensity values. higher value indicates higher contrast. image   automatically normalized necessary (.e., normalization range [0,   1]). color images, weighted average color channel's values   computed.

title: Image contrast — img_contrast
text: Peli, E. (1990). Contrast complex images. Journal   Optical Society America , 7, 2032–2040.   doi:10.1364/JOSAA.7.002032

title: Image self-similarity — img_self_similarity
text: img_self_similarity returns self-similarity image (.e., degree log-log power spectrum image falls slope -2). Higher values indicate higher image self-similarity.

title: Image self-similarity — img_self_similarity
code: img_self_similarity(img, full = FALSE, logplot = FALSE, raw = FALSE)

title: Image self-similarity — img_self_similarity
text: img image form matrix array numeric values, preferably square size. input square, bilinear resizing square size performed using OpenImageR package. Use e.g. img_read() read image file R. full logical. full frequency range used interpolation? (default: FALSE) logplot logical. log-log power spectrum image plotted? (default: FALSE) raw logical. raw value regression slope returned? (default: FALSE)

title: Image self-similarity — img_self_similarity
text: numeric value (self-similarity)

title: Image self-similarity — img_self_similarity
text: function takes (square) array matrix numeric integer   values representing image input returns self-similarity   image. Self-similarity computed via slope log-log power   spectrum using OLS. slope near -2 indicates fractal-like   properties (see Redies et al., 2007; Simoncelli & Olshausen, 2001). Thus,   value self-similarity return function calculated   self-similarity = abs(slope + 2) * (-1). , measure   reaches maximum value 0 slope -2, deviation -2   results negative values negative higher deviation   -2. color images, weighted average color channel's   values computed (cf. Mayer & Landwehr 2018). Per default, frequency range betwen 10 256 cycles per image   used interpolation. Computation full range can set via   parameter full = TRUE. logplot set TRUE log-log plot power   spectrum additionally shown. package ggplot2 installed   plot includes slope OLS regression. Note option   currently implemented grayscale images. possible get raw regression slope (instead transformed   value indicates self-similarity) using option raw =   TRUE. color images, weighed average color channel's values   computed.

title: Image self-similarity — img_self_similarity
text: function inspired Matlab's sfPlot (Diederick C. Niehorster).

title: Image self-similarity — img_self_similarity
text: Mayer, S. & Landwehr, J, R. (2018). Quantifying Visual Aesthetics   Based Processing Fluency Theory: Four Algorithmic Measures   Antecedents Aesthetic Preferences. Psychology Aesthetics,   Creativity, Arts, 12(4), 399–431.   doi:10.1037/aca0000187 Redies, C., Hasenstein, J., & Denzler, J. (2007). Fractal-like   image statistics visual art: Similarity natural scenes. Spatial   Vision, 21, 137–148.   doi:10.1163/156856807782753921 Simoncelli, E. P., & Olshausen, B. . (2001). Natural image   statistics neural representation. Annual Review Neuroscience,   24, 1193–1216.   doi:10.1146/annurev.neuro.24.1.1193

title: Image self-similarity — img_self_similarity
code: # Example image with high self-similarity: romanesco romanesco <- img_read(system.file("example_images", "romanesco.jpg", package = "imagefluency")) # # display image grid::grid.raster(romanesco) # # get self-similarity img_self_similarity(romanesco) #> [1] -0.03157856  # Example image with low self-similarity: office office <- img_read(system.file("example_images", "office.jpg", package = "imagefluency")) # # display image grid::grid.raster(office)  # # get self-similarity img_self_similarity(office) #> [1] -1.217377

title: Image simplicity — img_simplicity
text: img_simplicity returns simplicity image 1 minus complexity image. Higher values indicated higher image simplicity.

title: Image simplicity — img_simplicity
code: img_simplicity(imgfile, algorithm = "zip", rotate = FALSE)

title: Image simplicity — img_simplicity
text: imgfile Either character string containing path image file (URL) image form matrix (grayscale image) array (color image) numeric values representing pre-loaded image (e.g. using img_read()). algorithm Character string specifies image compression algorithm use. Currently implemented zip deflate compression, jpg, gif, png. rotate logical. compressed file size rotated image also computed? (see details)

title: Image simplicity — img_simplicity
text: numeric value: 1 minus ratio compressed divided   uncompressed file size (.e., compression rate)

title: Image simplicity — img_simplicity
text: Image simplicity calculated 1 minus ratio   compressed uncompressed file size (.e., compression rate). Values   can range 0 (compression possible, thus extremely complex image)   almost 1 (virtually completely compressed image, thus extremely simple   image). Different compression algorithms implemented. details, see   img_complexity.

title: Image simplicity — img_simplicity
text: Donderi, D. C. (2006). Visual complexity: Review.   Psychological Bulletin, 132, 73–97.   doi:10.1037/0033-2909.132.1.73 Forsythe, ., Nadal, M., Sheehy, N., Cela-Conde, C. J., & Sawey,   M. (2011). Predicting Beauty: Fractal Dimension Visual Complexity   Art. British Journal Psychology, 102, 49–70.   doi:10.1348/000712610X498958 Mayer, S. & Landwehr, J, R. (2018). Quantifying Visual Aesthetics   Based Processing Fluency Theory: Four Algorithmic Measures   Antecedents Aesthetic Preferences. Psychology Aesthetics,   Creativity, Arts, 12(4), 399–431.   doi:10.1037/aca0000187

title: Image simplicity — img_simplicity
code: # Example image with low simplicity: trees trees <- img_read(system.file("example_images", "trees.jpg", package = "imagefluency")) # # display image grid::grid.raster(trees) # # get simplicity img_simplicity(trees) #> [1] 0.1050314  # Example image with high simplicity: sky sky <- img_read(system.file("example_images", "sky.jpg", package = "imagefluency")) # # display image grid::grid.raster(sky)  # # get simplicity img_simplicity(sky) #> [1] 0.5800746

title: Image symmetry — img_symmetry
text: img_symmetry returns vertical horizontal mirror symmetry image. Higher values indicate higher image symmetry.

title: Image symmetry — img_symmetry
code: img_symmetry(img, vertical = TRUE, horizontal = TRUE, ...)

title: Image symmetry — img_symmetry
text: img image form matrix array numeric values. Use e.g. img_read() read image file R. vertical logical. vertical symmetry computed? (default: TRUE) horizontal logical. horizontal symmetry computed? (default: TRUE) ... options: shift_range shift mirror axis, per_channel switch maximal per channel vs. per image symmetry (see details).

title: Image symmetry — img_symmetry
text: named vector numeric values (vertical horizontal symmetry)

title: Image symmetry — img_symmetry
text: function returns vertical horizontal mirror symmetry   image img. Symmetry values can range 0 (symmetrical)   1 (fully symmetrical). vertical horizontal set   FALSE vertical horizontal symmetry computed,   respectively. perceptual mirror axis necessarily exactly middle   picture, function estimates first step several symmetry values   different positions mirror axis. end, mirror axis   automatically shifted 5% (default) image width left   right (case vertical symmetry; analogously   horizontal symmetry). second step, overall symmetry score   computed maximum symmetry scores given different mirror   axes. See Mayer & Landwehr (2018) details. Advanced users can change shift range optional parameter   shift_range, takes numeric decimal input. default   shift_range = 0.05 (.e., 5%). color images, default first maximal symmetry score (  explained ) obtained per color channel (parameter per_channel   = TRUE). Subsequently, weighted average color channel's   maximal score computed image's overall symmetry. Advanced users   can reverse order setting per_channel = FALSE. results   first computing weighted averages position mirror   axis separately, afterwards finding maximal overall symmetry score.

title: Image symmetry — img_symmetry
text: Mayer, S. & Landwehr, J, R. (2018). Quantifying Visual Aesthetics   Based Processing Fluency Theory: Four Algorithmic Measures   Antecedents Aesthetic Preferences. Psychology Aesthetics,   Creativity, Arts, 12(4), 399–431.   doi:10.1037/aca0000187

title: Image symmetry — img_symmetry
code: # Example image with high vertical symmetry: rails rails <- img_read(system.file("example_images", "rails.jpg", package = "imagefluency")) # # display image grid::grid.raster(rails) # # get symmetry img_symmetry(rails) #>   vertical horizontal  #>  0.9550184  0.1230524   # Example image with low vertical symmetry: bridge bridge <- img_read(system.file("example_images", "bridge.jpg", package = "imagefluency")) # # display image grid::grid.raster(bridge)  # # get symmetry img_symmetry(bridge) #>   vertical horizontal  #>  0.4802899  0.2525909

what: Typicality of images relative to each other — img_typicality
title: Typicality of images relative to each other — img_typicality
text: img_typicality returns visual typicality list images relative . Higher values indicate larger typicality.

title: Typicality of images relative to each other — img_typicality
code: img_typicality(imglist, rescale = NULL)

title: Typicality of images relative to each other — img_typicality
text: imglist list arrays matrices numeric values. Use e.g. img_read() read image files R (see example). rescale numeric. Rescales images prior computing typicality scores (per default rescaling performed). Rescaling performed OpenImageR's resizeImage function (bilinear rescaling)

title: Typicality of images relative to each other — img_typicality
text: named matrix numeric values (typicality scores)

title: Typicality of images relative to each other — img_typicality
text: function returns visual typicality list image   arrays matrices imglist relative . Values can range   -1 (inversely typical) 0 (typical) 1 (perfectly typical).   , higher absolute values indicate larger typicality. typicality score computed correlation particular image   average representation images, .e. mean images.   color images, weighted average color channel's values   computed. images different dimensions automatically   resized smallest height width. Rescaling images prior computing typicality scores can   specified optional rescaling parameter (must numeric value).   users need rescaling can use default (rescale   = NULL). See Mayer & Landwehr (2018) details.

title: Typicality of images relative to each other — img_typicality
text: Mayer, S. & Landwehr, J. R. (2018). Objective measures design   typicality. Design Studies, 54, 146–161.   doi:10.1016/j.destud.2017.09.004

title: Typicality of images relative to each other — img_typicality
code: # Example images depicting valleys: valley_green, valley_white # Example image depicting fireworks: fireworks valley_green <- img_read(     system.file("example_images", "valley_green.jpg", package = "imagefluency")   ) valley_white <- img_read(     system.file("example_images", "valley_white.jpg", package = "imagefluency")   ) fireworks <- img_read(     system.file("example_images", "fireworks.jpg", package = "imagefluency")   ) # # display images grid::grid.raster(valley_green) grid::grid.raster(valley_white) grid::grid.raster(fireworks)   # create image set as list imglist <- list(fireworks, valley_green, valley_white)  # get typicality img_typicality(imglist) #>      [,1] #> img1  .40 #> img2  .72 #> img3  .75

title: RGB to Gray Conversion — rgb2gray
text: rgb2gray transforms colors RGB space (red/green/blue) matrix grayscale values.

title: RGB to Gray Conversion — rgb2gray
code: rgb2gray(img)

title: RGB to Gray Conversion — rgb2gray
text: img 3-dimensional array numeric integer values

title: RGB to Gray Conversion — rgb2gray
text: matrix grayscale values.

title: RGB to Gray Conversion — rgb2gray
text: function takes 3-dimensional array   numeric integer values input (img)   returns matrix grayscale values output.   grayscale values computed GRAY = 0.2989 *   RED + 0.5870 * GREEN + 0.1140 * BLUE. array   fourth dimension (.e., alpha channel), fourth   dimension ignored.

title: RGB to Gray Conversion — rgb2gray
code: # construct a sample RGB image as array of random integers imgRed <- matrix(runif(100, min = 0, max = 255), 10, 10) imgGreen <- matrix(runif(100, min = 0, max = 255), 10, 10) imgBlue <- matrix(runif(100, min = 0, max = 255), 10, 10) imgColor <- array(c(imgRed, imgGreen, imgBlue), dim = c(10, 10, 3))  # convert to gray img <- rgb2gray(imgColor)

what: Matrix or Array Rotation by 90 Degrees — rotate90
title: Matrix or Array Rotation by 90 Degrees — rotate90
text: Matrix Array Rotation 90 Degrees

title: Matrix or Array Rotation by 90 Degrees — rotate90
code: rotate90(img, direction = "positive")

title: Matrix or Array Rotation by 90 Degrees — rotate90
text: img array matrix direction direction rotation 90 degrees. value can "positive" (default) "negative". Aliases "counterclockwise" "clockwise", respectively.

title: Matrix or Array Rotation by 90 Degrees — rotate90
text: array matrix (rotated 90 degrees)

title: Matrix or Array Rotation by 90 Degrees — rotate90
text: function takes array matrix input   object (img) returns object rotated   90 degrees. Per default, rotation done   mathematically positive direction (.e.,   counterclockwise). Clockwise rotation (.e.,   mathematically negative) can specified passing   value "negative" direction   argument.

title: Matrix or Array Rotation by 90 Degrees — rotate90
code: # sample matrix img <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) img #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    1    4 #> [2,]    2    5 #> [3,]    3    6  rotate90(img) # counterclockwise #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    4    5    6 #> [2,]    1    2    3 rotate90(img, direction = "negative") # clockwise #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    3    2    1 #> [2,]    6    5    4

title: Run imagefluency app — run_imagefluency

title: imagefluency 1.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-03-31 redesigned Shiny dashboard multi-image upload, integrated results, image comparison, CSV export added optional collapse-based speedups self-similarity, symmetry, typicality computations, retaining base R fallbacks made complexity tests robust across platforms updated test suite modern testthat conventions fixed img_complexity() normalization array inputs outside [0, 1] range avoid platform-dependent results
